flashcards.front: What is an Entity Set in ER Diagrams?
flashcards.back: An entity set is a collection of entities of the same type that share common attributes. For example, all students in a university form a 'Student' entity set. Entities are represented as rectangles in ER diagrams.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between Simple, Composite, and Derived Attributes?
flashcards.back: Simple attributes cannot be divided (e.g., age); Composite attributes can be broken into subparts (e.g., address → street, city); Derived attributes are calculated from other attributes (e.g., age from date of birth). Derived attributes are shown with dashed ovals.

flashcards.front: Define Cardinality Ratios in ER Diagrams with examples.
flashcards.back: Cardinality ratios specify the number of entities an entity can associate with. Types: (1:1) one-to-one (one student has one ID card), (1:N) one-to-many (one department has many employees), (M:N) many-to-many (students enroll in many courses).

flashcards.front: What are Total and Partial Participation constraints?
flashcards.back: Total participation (existence dependency) means every entity in the set must participate in the relationship (shown by double lines). Partial participation means participation is optional (shown by single lines). Example: Every student must enroll (total) but not all students join clubs (partial).

flashcards.front: What is a Weak Entity Set? How is it represented?
flashcards.back: A weak entity depends on another entity for identification and lacks a primary key of its own. It has a partial key that uniquely identifies related entities. Weak entities are represented by double rectangles, and their relationships by double diamonds.

flashcards.front: Explain Binary, Ternary, and Recursive relationships.
flashcards.back: Binary relationships involve two entity sets (most common); Ternary involve three entity sets (one relationship connects three entities); Recursive (unary) relationships connect an entity set to itself (e.g., employee manages employee). Ternary relationships are drawn as diamonds connecting three entities.

flashcards.front: What are the key types in ER Diagrams?
flashcards.back: Key types include: Primary Key (unique identifier, underlined), Candidate Key (all minimal superkeys), Foreign Key (references primary key of another entity), and Composite Key (multiple attributes forming a key). Keys are essential for maintaining entity uniqueness.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between Entity and Relationship sets?
flashcards.back: Entity sets are collections of entities (objects), represented by rectangles. Relationship sets are associations among entities, represented by diamonds. Each relationship has a name and may have descriptive attributes (e.g., 'Works_In' relationship between Employee and Department with 'duration' as attribute).
